title: The Fourth Sunday of Advent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## December 22, 2019 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

---

## LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Fourth Sunday of Advent, occurring just three days before Christmas Eve. The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this Sunday typically include 2 Samuel 7:1-11, 16 (God's covenant with David); Romans 16:25-27 (Paul's doxology); and Luke 1:26-38 (the Annunciation to Mary). The sermon falls in the final moments of Advent, the liturgical season of waiting and anticipation, with the full celebration of the Incarnation arriving within 72 hours.

## EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

In late 2019, the Episcopal Church remained internally fractious following the July 2018 General Convention's reaffirmation of LGBTQ+ inclusion and the ongoing debates over pastoral provisions for traditionalist parishes. The Diocese of Colorado had been relatively stable under Bishop Robert O'Neill (who served 2008-2020), though national tensions around sexuality and biblical interpretation continued to simmer. No major Episcopal denominational news broke specifically during the week of December 16-22, 2019.

## DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in its typical pre-Christmas period with holiday lights and seasonal events dominating local coverage. The week prior (mid-December) saw typical winter weather patterns for Colorado; no major weather events or civic crises dominated headlines. Downtown Denver, where Saint John's Cathedral is located on 17th Avenue in the Capitol Hill area, would have been bustling with holiday shopping and seasonal activities.

## NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The nation was in the final days before Christmas under the shadow of President Trump's first impeachment, which had concluded its House Judiciary Committee hearings on December 13 and would move to the full House floor for a vote on December 18-19 (resulting in two articles of impeachment). The stock market remained strong, and holiday shopping season was in full swing. Democratic primary campaigning for the 2020 presidential election was heating up, with Iowa caucuses scheduled for early February.

## INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Syrian conflict continued with Russian and Turkish military forces involved in the northwest; Bashar al-Assad remained in control of most of the country. The Hong Kong pro-democracy protests, which had dominated news for months, were entering a quieter phase as 2019 wound down, though tensions remained high following the November elections. Brexit remained unresolved, with Boris Johnson's government having called for a general election in December 2019 that solidified his position to pursue a departure from the EU.

## CULTURAL MOMENT

America was in full Christmas mode with a pervasive sense of seasonal normalcy masking deeper political polarization from impeachment. The zeitgeist combined holiday cheer with election-year anxiety and divisiveness. Popular cultural touchstones included the final season of "Game of Thrones" (which had aired in spring, still being discussed), Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker opening on December 20, and the trending awareness of climate activism led by young voices like Greta Thunberg, who had been named TIME's Person of the Year just days earlier on December 11.
